teaching_card.title: Exponents and Radicals (Medium)
teaching_card.body: Exponent rules let you combine powers. Multiplying powers with the same base adds exponents; dividing subtracts them; a power of a power multiplies them. A radical is a fractional exponent.
key_points: (aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ (multiply for a power of a power).

screens.title: The three core rules
screens.body: Same base: multiplying ADDS exponents (xᵃ·xᵇ = xᵃ⁺ᵇ), dividing SUBTRACTS them (xᵃ ÷ xᵇ = xᵃ⁻ᵇ), and a power of a power MULTIPLIES them ((xᵃ)ᵇ = xᵃᵇ). The base never changes.
key_points: Power of a power -> multiply exponents.

screens.title: Negative and zero
screens.body: A zero exponent is 1 (x⁰ = 1). A negative exponent means a reciprocal: x⁻ⁿ = 1/xⁿ. So when subtracting exponents gives a negative, the answer is a fraction.

screens.title: Roots are fractional powers
screens.body: A radical is a fractional exponent: √x = x^(1/2), and ⁿ√(xᵏ) = x^(k/n). Rewriting roots as powers lets you use the same exponent rules to simplify.
key_points: Convert roots to powers, then apply the rules.

screens.title: Try the moves
screens.body: (2³)² ÷ 2⁸ = 2⁶ ÷ 2⁸ = 2⁻² = 1/2² = 1/4. Apply power-of-a-power, then subtract for the division, then read the negative exponent as a reciprocal.
